headline: FEEDERS MOVE HIGHER 3.2%, CATTLE COMPLEX SNAPS HIGHER
subheadline: Cargill lockout pressure finally breaks, live cattle follow feeders up 1.6% in the biggest session since May.
lead: Feeder cattle closed $353.38, up 3.2% in the biggest session since the Cargill lockout began. Live cattle followed with a 1.6% gain to $241.68, both contracts bouncing hard off Wednesday's lows on reports the Fort Morgan situation might finally resolve. The grain liquidation that dominated the week stayed liquid-corn lost another penny to $4.21-but cattle just answered the week's question: liquidation finished, weather takes control next week.
the_takeaway: Cattle lockout pressure breaks, feeders lead the complex higher.
teaser: Feeders move higher 3.2% as Cargill lockout pressure finally cracks.

one_number.unit: days since the Cargill lockout began
one_number.context: Fort Morgan and Schuyler plants locked out 1,700 workers on May 19, removing 6,000 head of daily processing capacity. Today's 3.2% feeder move higher suggests the market believes resolution is coming. The lockout created a processing bottleneck that drove feeders down 8% while live cattle held-today that spread snapped back hard.

yesterdays_call.summary: Called that cattle liquidation was exhausted and both contracts would bounce hard off Wednesday's lows.
yesterdays_call.note: The bounce came bigger and cleaner than expected, feeders leading live by 160 basis points.

sections.title: CATTLE COMPLEX RUNS HIGHER
sections.body: Feeders **ran 3.2%** to $353.38, the biggest session since the Cargill lockout began eleven days ago. Live cattle followed with a 1.6% gain to $241.68, both contracts reversing Wednesday's breakdown in convincing fashion. The **lockout premium is deflating**-reports suggest Fort Morgan negotiations are advancing, though no official resolution yet. Funds that sold the processing bottleneck are covering into strength. The feeder/live ratio tightened to 1.46 from 1.48 Wednesday, normal seasonal behavior when processing constraints ease.
sections.bottom_line: Processing bottleneck premium unwinding, both contracts bouncing hard.

sections.title: GRAINS FINISH LIQUIDATION WEEK
sections.body: Corn closed $4.21, down another penny in the **fourth straight session** of fund selling. Soybeans held $11.28, off just a penny, but the Dec contract lost 1.5 cents as the carry widened again. Wheat bucked the trend, gaining 2 cents to $5.83 on short covering ahead of the weekend. **Open interest dropped 15,000 contracts** across the grain complex Friday-somebody finished taking profits. The planting season behind-schedule premium is gone, replaced by normal June weather watch.
sections.catalyst: No specific catalyst, fund liquidation completing weekly selling cycle
sections.bottom_line: Fund selling cycle completes, normal June weather watch begins Monday.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: liquidation extended exactly as flagged, wheat showing first resistance

sections.title: CRUDE LOSES IRAN PREMIUM
sections.body: WTI crude closed $92.86, down 30 cents as **Iran-Hormuz tensions continue easing** on diplomatic progress. The strait premium that built since early April is deflating methodically-crude lost 19% in May and gave back another 0.3% today. Natural gas dropped 1.1% to $3.31 on storage builds and mild weather forecasts. Energy's the mirror image of cattle: geopolitical premium deflating while processing premiums (refineries running hard into summer) firm the crack spreads.
sections.catalyst: Continued Iran-Hormuz diplomatic progress via Swiss intermediaries
sections.bottom_line: Geopolitical premium keeps deflating, crack spreads firm on refinery runs.

outside_the_pit.title: New World screwworm confirmed in south Texas calf
outside_the_pit.body: USDA confirmed the parasitic fly 31 miles from the US border, triggering livestock movement restrictions. Agriculture Secretary Rollins says cattle movement won't be 'compromised,' but the discovery adds regulatory uncertainty to an already volatile cattle market.

outside_the_pit.title: EU suspends fertilizer tariffs for one year
outside_the_pit.body: European Commission drops all import tariffs on nitrogen fertilizers to cut farmer input costs by €60 million. The move signals continued global pressure on fertilizer supply chains, potentially affecting US nitrogen pricing as European demand shifts.

outside_the_pit.title: House passes FY2027 Ag appropriations along party lines
outside_the_pit.body: The bill passed 213-210 Thursday with five Republicans crossing party lines. The vote sets up Senate negotiations on farm program funding levels and rural development priorities heading into the next growing season.

spread_to_watch.label: Feeder cattle / live cattle ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: The ratio gained during the Cargill lockout as processing bottlenecks hurt feeders more than live cattle. Today's tightening says the market believes the processing constraint is easing. Watch for a move toward the seasonal 1.44 if lockout resolution becomes official.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firms on ethanol maintenance return
basis.body: **Eastern Belt corn basis tightening** as ethanol plants finish scheduled maintenance and restart operations. Producers east of the Mississippi with old-crop inventory have a window the futures board alone isn't pricing. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with the seasonal as river system opens transportation capacity.

weekly_thread.question: Will the grain liquidation finish before weather takes control of the story?
weekly_thread.status_text: The answer is yes. Fund selling cycle completed this week with 15,000 contracts of open interest liquidated Friday. Normal June weather watch begins Monday-the calendar hands control back to Mother Nature.

the_more_you_know.title: Why lockout premiums snap back faster than they build
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 3.2% feeder move higher on lockout resolution reports shows why processing bottlenecks create asymmetric risk. The Cargill Fort Morgan lockout took eleven days to drive feeders down 8%, but just one session of resolution hope brought back 3.2%. Markets price worst-case scenarios into processing disruptions, then unwind the premium violently when normal operations return. The 1,700 locked-out workers represent 2% of weekly US slaughter capacity-small in aggregate, massive in local markets where the plants operate.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn above 50% planted keeps normal weather watch, below 45% adds prevent-plant premium
Cargill Fort Morgan lockout resolution announcement
Weekly export sales: soy under 400K MT keeps fund pressure on beans

daily_quote.text: Don't count your chickens before they hatch.

source_summary: CBOT/CME settlement prices, USDA reports, industry trade publications
